question: A volume of 50.00 mL of a weak acid of unknown concentration is titrated with 0.10 M solution of NaOH. The equivalence point is reached after 39.30 mL of NaOH solution has been added. At the half equivalence point (19.65 mL) the pH is 4.85. Thus, initial concentration of the acid and its pKa values are
options:
$$[HA]$$ $$p{K_a}$$
0.1 M 4.85

$$[HA]$$ $$p{K_a}$$
0.079 M 4.85

$$[HA]$$ $$p{K_a}$$
0.1 M 3.70

$$[HA]$$ $$p{K_a}$$
0.097 M 2.93
solution:
From molarity equation,

M1V1 = M2V2

M1 $$\times$$ 50.0 = 0.10 $$\times$$ 39.30

$$\therefore$$ $${M_1} = {{0.10 \times 39.30} \over {50}} = 0.0786$$ M

When half equivalence point is reached,

$$[HA] = [{A^ - }]$$

$$pH = p{K_a} + \log {{[{A^ - }]} \over {[HA]}}$$

$$pH = p{K_a} = 4.85$$

question:
A(g) $$\to$$ P(g) + Q(g) + R(g),

Follow first order kinetics with a half-life of 69.3 s at 500$$^\circ$$C. Starting from the gas 'A' an container at 500$$^\circ$$C and at a pressure of 0.4 atm, the total pressure of the system after 230 s will be
solution:
Pt = 0.4 $$-$$ x + x + x + x = 0.4 + 2x or x = $${{{P_t} - 0.4} \over 2}$$

PA = P0 $$-$$ x = P0 $$-$$ $${{{P_t} - 0.4} \over 2}$$ = $${{2 \times 0.4 - {P_t} + 0.4} \over 2} = {{1.2 - {P_t}} \over 2}$$

From, $$k = {{2.303} \over t}\log {{{P_0}} \over {{P_A}}}$$

$${{0.693} \over {{t_{1/2}}}} = {{2.303} \over {230}}\log \left( {{{0.4 \times 2} \over {1.2 - {P_t}}}} \right)$$

$${{0.693} \over {{t_{1/2}}}} = {{2.303} \over {230}}\log \left( {{{0.8} \over {1.2 - {P_t}}}} \right)$$

$$\log {{0.8} \over {1.2 - {P_t}}} = {{0.693 \times 230} \over {69.3 \times 2.303}} = 0.9987$$

$${{0.8} \over {1.2 - {P_t}}} = {10^{0.9987}}$$

$$0.8 = (10)(1.2 - {P_t})$$

$$0.8 = 12 - 10{P_t}$$

$$10{P_t} = 12 - 0.8$$

$$10{P_t} = 11.2$$

$${P_t} = {{11.2} \over {10}} = 1.12$$ atm

question:
For the following reaction

2A + 3B $$\to$$ 3C + 4D

expression for rate of reaction is
solution:
The rate of a chemical reaction can be expressed in terms of the rate of change of concentration of any reactant or product. According to the given reaction:

$$ 2A + 3B \to 3C + 4D $$

The rate of this reaction can be expressed as the rate of disappearance of the reactants A or B, or as the rate of appearance of the products C or D. Since A is being consumed, its concentration will decrease over time and because B, C, and D are all involved in the reaction in different stoichiometric ratios, their rates of consumption or production will be proportional to their stoichiometric coefficients.

The general relation between the rate of the reaction and the rate of change of concentration of any species is given by:

$$ \text{Rate} = -\frac{1}{\text{stoichiometric coefficient}} \frac{d[\text{reactant}]}{dt} = \frac{1}{\text{stoichiometric coefficient}} \frac{d[\text{product}]}{dt} $$

For reactant A with a stoichiometric coefficient of 2:

$$ \text{Rate} = -\frac{1}{2} \frac{d[A]}{dt} $$

For reactant B with a stoichiometric coefficient of 3:

$$ \text{Rate} = -\frac{1}{3} \frac{d[B]}{dt} $$

For product C with a stoichiometric coefficient of 3:

$$ \text{Rate} = \frac{1}{3} \frac{d[C]}{dt} $$

For product D with a stoichiometric coefficient of 4:

$$ \text{Rate} = \frac{1}{4} \frac{d[D]}{dt} $$

So the correct options that match the expression for the rate of reaction based on the appearance of products C and D are:

Option C: $$ {1 \over 3}{{d[C]} \over {dt}} $$

Option D: $$ {1 \over 4}{{d[D]} \over {dt}} $$

Note that when expressing the rate with respect to the reactants (A and B), the rate must include a negative sign because their concentrations decrease as the reaction proceeds. For products (C and D), the rate is positive because their concentrations increase.

question: For the reaction $ 2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3} $, the rate of disappearance of $ \mathrm{O}_{2} $ is $ 2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}{ }^{-1} $ $ \mathrm{s}^{-1} $. The rate appearance of $ \mathrm{SO}_{3} $ is
solution:
For the reaction $ 2 \mathrm{SO}_{2} + \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3} $, we can determine the rate of appearance of $\mathrm{SO}_{3}$ from the given information.

Given:

Rate of disappearance of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ is $2 \times 10^{-4} \, \mathrm{mol} \, \mathrm{L}^{-1} \, \mathrm{s}^{-1}$.

The reaction rate expressions can be set up as:

$ \text{Rate of reaction} = -\frac{1}{2} \frac{d[\mathrm{SO}_{2}]}{dt} = -\frac{d[\mathrm{O}_{2}]}{dt} = \frac{1}{2} \frac{d[\mathrm{SO}_{3}]}{dt} $

To find the rate of appearance of $\mathrm{SO}_{3}$, we use the stoichiometry of the reaction. Since one mole of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ produces two moles of $\mathrm{SO}_{3}$, the rate of appearance of $\mathrm{SO}_{3}$ is twice the rate of disappearance of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$.

$ \frac{d[\mathrm{SO}_{3}]}{dt} = 2 \times \left(-\frac{d[\mathrm{O}_{2}]}{dt}\right) = 2 \times (2 \times 10^{-4}) $

Calculating this gives:

$ \frac{d[\mathrm{SO}_{3}]}{dt} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \, \mathrm{mol} \, \mathrm{L}^{-1} \, \mathrm{s}^{-1} $

Thus, the rate of appearance of $\mathrm{SO}_{3}$ is $4 \times 10^{-4} \, \mathrm{mol} \, \mathrm{L}^{-1} \, \mathrm{s}^{-1}$.

question: If for a first order reaction, the value of $ A $ and $ E_{a} $ are $ 4 \times 10^{13} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} $ and $ 98.6 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} $ respectively, then at what temperature will its half life period be 10 minutes?
solution:
According to the Arrhenius equation:

$ \log k = \log A - \frac{E_a}{2303RT} $

For a first-order reaction, the rate constant $ k $ can be expressed in terms of half-life ($ t_{1/2} $) as:

$ k = \frac{0.693}{t_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{600 \text{ s}} = 1.1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1} $

Next, we substitute the value of $ k $ into the Arrhenius equation:

$ \log \left(1.1 \times 10^{-3}\right) = \log \left(4 \times 10^{13}\right) - \frac{98.6 \times 10^3}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times T} $

Solving for $ T $, we find:

$ T = 311.15 \text{ K} $

question:
For the reaction,

$$ 2 \mathrm{SO}_2+\mathrm{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_3 $$

if the rate of disappearance of $\mathrm{O}_2$ is $2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$, determine the rate of appearance of $\mathrm{SO}_3$.
solution:
From the balanced reaction,

$$ 2 \mathrm{SO}_2+\mathrm{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_3 $$

Using stoichiometry,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Rate of disapperance of } \mathrm{O}_2=2 \times 10^{-1} \mathrm{molL}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\,\frac{-d\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]}{d t}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{d\left[\mathrm{SO}_3\right]}{d t} \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\, \frac{d\left[\mathrm{SO}_3\right]}{d t}=2 \times\left[\frac{-d\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]}{d t}\right] \end{aligned} $$

Rate of appearance of

$$ \mathrm{SO}_3=2 \times\left(2 \times 10^{-1}\right)=4 \times 10^{-1} \mathrm{molL}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} $$

question: If for a first-order reaction, the frequency factor $A=4 \times 10^{13} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ and activation energy $E_a=98.6 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, at what temperature will the half-life be 10 minutes?
solution:
Half-life for first-order reaction,

$$ t_{1 / 2}=0.693 / \mathrm{k} $$

Given, $t_{1 / 2}=600 \mathrm{~s}$

$$ k=\frac{0.693}{600} \approx 1.155 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} $$

Using Arrhenius,

$$ k=A e^{\left(-E_0 / R T\right)} $$

Take $\log$ both sides and solve,

$$ \ln k=\ln (A)-\frac{E_a}{R T} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Substituting the values, }\\ &\begin{aligned} \ln \left(1.155 \times 10^{-3}\right) & =\ln \left(4 \times 10^{13}\right)-\left(\frac{98600}{R T}\right) \\ & \approx 311.49 k \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

question:
The following experimental rate data were obtained for a reaction carried out at $$25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$

$$A_{(g)}+B_{(g)} \rightarrow C_{(g)}+D_{(g)}$$

Initial $$\mathrm{[A_{(g)}]/mol~dm^{-3}}$$ Initial $$\mathrm{[B_{(g)}]/mol~dm^{-3}}$$ Initial rate/mol $$\mathrm{dm^{-3}s^{-1}}$$
$$3.0 \times 10^{-2}$$ $$2.0 \times 10^{-2}$$ $$1.89 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$3.0 \times 10^{-2}$$ $$4.0 \times 10^{-2}$$ $$1.89 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$6.0 \times 10^{-2}$$ $$4.0 \times 10^{-2}$$ $$7.56 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\text { What are the orders with respect to } \mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{g})} \text { and } \mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})} \text { ? }$$
options:
Order with respect to $$\mathrm{A_{(g)}}$$ Order with respect to $$\mathrm{B_{(g)}}$$
Zero Second

Order with respect to $$\mathrm{A_{(g)}}$$ Order with respect to $$\mathrm{B_{(g)}}$$
First Zero

Order with respect to $$\mathrm{A_{(g)}}$$ Order with respect to $$\mathrm{B_{(g)}}$$
Second Zero

Order with respect to $$\mathrm{A_{(g)}}$$ Order with respect to $$\mathrm{B_{(g)}}$$
Second First
solution:
To determine the order of the reaction with respect to each reactant ($$\mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{g})}$$ and $$\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})}$$), we'll analyze the rate data from the table:

Initial Rates and Concentrations :

1. $$[A_{(g)}] = 3.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}, [B_{(g)}] = 2.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}, \text{rate} = 1.89 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}\text{s}^{-1}$$

2. $$[A_{(g)}] = 3.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}, [B_{(g)}] = 4.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}, \text{rate} = 1.89 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}\text{s}^{-1}$$

3. $$[A_{(g)}] = 6.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}, [B_{(g)}] = 4.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}, \text{rate} = 7.56 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}\text{s}^{-1}$$

Comparing Experiments 1 and 2 (to determine the order with respect to $$\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})}$$):

- The concentration of $$\mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{g})}$$ is constant.

- The concentration of $$\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})}$$ is doubled.

- The rate remains the same.

This suggests that changing the concentration of $$\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})}$$ has no effect on the rate, meaning the order with respect to $$\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})}$$ is zero.

Comparing Experiments 2 and 3 (to determine the order with respect to $$\mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{g})}$$):

- The concentration of $$\mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{g})}$$ is doubled ($$3.0 \times 10^{-2}$$ to $$6.0 \times 10^{-2}$$).

- The concentration of $$\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})}$$ remains constant.

- The rate increases by a factor of 4 ($$1.89 \times 10^{-4}$$ to $$7.56 \times 10^{-4}$$).

Because doubling $$\mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{g})}$$ results in the rate being quadrupled, it suggests that the order with respect to $$\mathrm{A}_{(\mathrm{g})}$$ is two (second order).

The correct orders are:

Order with respect to $$\mathrm{A_{(g)}}$$: Second

Order with respect to $$\mathrm{B_{(g)}}$$: Zero

Hence, the correct option is C :

Order with respect to $$\mathrm{A_{(g)}}$$ Order with respect to $$\mathrm{B_{(g)}}$$
Second Zero

question:
Assertion (A): For a zero order reaction the unit of rate constant and rate of reaction are same.

Reason (R) : Rate of reaction for zero order reaction is independent of concentration of reactant.
options:
Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason (R) is the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) and true, but Reason (R) is not the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
Assertion (A) is true, but Reason (R) is false.
Assertion (A) is false, but Reason (R) is true.

question: What happens to the rate constant $$k$$ and activation energy $$E_a$$ as the temperature of a chemical reaction is increased? Justify.
solution:
Rate constant increases with the increase in temperature because rate of the reaction increases. The rate of the reaction becomes doubled after every ten degree rise in temperature.

The activation energy also increases with increase in temperature because kinetic energy of the molecules increases their colliding frequency will be very high and activation energy increases.

question:
(a) For the reaction

$$2 \mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_{5(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{NO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \text { at } 318 \mathrm{~K}$$

Calculate the rate of reaction if rate of disappearance of $$\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_{5(\mathrm{~g})}$$ is $$1.4 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$$.

(b) For a first order reaction derive the relationship $$\mathbf{t}_{99 \%}=\mathbf{2} \mathbf{t}_{90 \%}$$
solution:
$$\text { (a) } 2 \mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5 \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{NO}_2+\mathrm{O}_2$$

$$\frac{-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=+\frac{\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{~d}\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=+\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}$$

$$=2 \times \frac{1}{4} \times 1.4 \times 10^{-3}$$ [Rate of disappearance $$=1.4 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$$

$$=0.7 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$$

(b) For a first order of reaction

$$\begin{aligned} & t=\frac{2.303}{K} \log \frac{a}{a-x} \\ & t_{99 \%}=\frac{2.303}{K} \log \frac{100}{1} \\ & =\frac{2.303}{K} \log 100 \\ & =\frac{2.303 \times 2}{K}=\frac{4.606}{K} \\ & \text { and } t_{90 \%}=\frac{2.303}{K} \log \frac{a}{a-x} \\ & =\frac{2.303}{K} \log 10=\frac{2.303}{K} \\ & \frac{t_{99 \%}}{t_{90 \%}}=2 \\ & t_{99 \%}=2 t_{90 \%} . \end{aligned}$$

question: The rate of a reaction increases sixteen times when the concentration of the reactant increases four times. The order of the reaction is
solution:
The rate of a reaction and its relation to the concentration of reactants can be described by the rate law:

$$ \text{Rate} = k [\text{A}]^n $$

where:

$\text{Rate}$ is the rate of the reaction,

$k$ is the rate constant,

$[\text{A}]$ is the concentration of the reactant, and

$n$ is the order of the reaction with respect to reactant A.

From the problem statement, the rate increases sixteen times when the concentration increases four times. This relationship can be expressed as:

$$ 16 \times \text{Rate}_0 = k (4[\text{A}_0])^n $$

where $\text{Rate}_0$ is the initial rate and $[\text{A}_0]$ is the initial concentration.

Since the initial rate is $\text{Rate}_0 = k [\text{A}_0]^n$, substituting and simplifying gives:

$$ 16 \times [\text{A}_0]^n = (4[\text{A}_0])^n $$

Dividing each side by $([\text{A}_0]^n)$:

$$ 16 = 4^n $$

Since $4^2 = 16$, it follows that $n = 2$.

Therefore, the order of the reaction is 2.0.

Option B is correct.

question:
Assertion (A): The units of rate constant of a zero order reaction and rate of reaction are the same.

Reason (R): In zero order reaction, the rate of reaction is independent of the concentration of reactants.
options:
Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason ( $R$ ) is the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
 Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true, but Reason ( $R$ ) is not the correct explanation of Assertion (A).
 Assertion (A) is true, but Reason (R) is false.
 Assertion (A) is false, but Reason (R) is true.
solution: The rate of a zero-order reaction does not depend on the concentration of the reactants. Therefore, the rate of a zero-order reaction is always equal to the rate constant of that specific reaction.

question: Show that in case of a first order reaction, the time taken for completion of $99 \%$ reaction is twice the time required for $90 \%$ completion of the reaction. $(\log 10=1)$
solution:
Case 1: If ' $t$ ' is the time required for $99 \%$ completion ' $a$ ' is the initial concentration of reactant then,

$$\begin{aligned} & x=99 \% \text { of } a \Rightarrow(a-x)=1 \% \text { of } a=\frac{1}{100} \times a=\frac{a}{100} \\ & t=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \left(\frac{a}{a-x}\right) \\ & =\frac{2.303}{k} \log \left(\frac{a \times 100}{a}\right) \\ & =\frac{2.303}{k} \log 10^2 \\ & \therefore \quad t=2\left[\frac{2.303}{k}\right] \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Case 2: If ' } \mathrm{t}^{\prime} \text { is the time required for } 90 \% \text { of completion }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \begin{aligned} \text { then } x=90 \% \text { of a } &(a-x)=1 \% \text { of } a=\frac{1}{100} \times a=\frac{a}{100} \\ & \qquad \begin{aligned} t & =\frac{2.303}{k} \log \left(\frac{a}{a-x}\right) \\ & =\frac{2.303}{k} \log \left(\frac{a \times 10}{a}\right) \end{aligned} \end{aligned} . \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} & =\frac{2.303}{k} \log 10 \\ \therefore & t =\left[\frac{2.303}{k}\right] \end{array}$$

Comparing: case 1 : case $2,2: 1$

So, the time required for the completion of $99 \%$ of reaction is twice the time required for $90 \%$ completion in a first order reaction.

question: The rate constant of a reaction quadruples when the temperature changes from 300 K to 320 K . Calculate the activation energy for this reaction. $\left[\log 2=0.30, \log 4=0.60,2.303 \mathrm{R}=19.15 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right]$
solution:
To determine the activation energy for a reaction where the rate constant quadruples as the temperature increases from 300 K to 320 K, we can use the Arrhenius equation.

Arrhenius Equation:

The equation used is:

$ \log \frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{E_a}{2.303 \times R} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right) $

Given that $\log 4 = 0.60$ and knowing $ R = 8.314 \, \text{J K}^{-1} \text{mol}^{-1} $, we substitute these into the equation:

$ \log 4 = \frac{E_a}{2.303 \times 8.314} \left( \frac{1}{300} - \frac{1}{320} \right) $

Rearranging to solve for $ E_a $:

$ E_a = \log 4 \times 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 320 \times \frac{300}{20} $

Substitute the given values:

$ E_a = 0.60 \times 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times \frac{320}{20} $

Finally, calculate:

$ E_a = 55152 \, \text{J/mol} $

Thus, the activation energy for the reaction is $ 55152 \, \text{J/mol} $.
